title: 11_churn_target_leakage.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Audit the Churn Model Before It Ships

The retention team's gradient-boosted churn model just posted a holdout AUC of 0.998, and the PM wants it wired into the win-back campaign tonight. Company policy stands in the way: no model ships until the feature-availability audit passes — every input column must be knowable at the moment the scoring service runs, for a live customer, today, before any outcome exists.

The audit cell is red. One of the four features fails the could-we-know-this-yet test, and it happens to be the one the model leans on hardest. Work out why the warehouse can produce a column for *training* that production can never produce for *scoring*, and make the notebook honest end to end.
initialCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

df = pd.read_csv("churn_snapshot.csv", parse_dates=["snapshot_date", "churn_date"])
horizon = df["churn_date"].fillna(df["snapshot_date"]) - df["snapshot_date"]
df["days_until_churn"] = horizon.dt.days
features = ["tenure_months", "monthly_spend", "support_tickets", "days_until_churn"]

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    df[features], df["churned"], test_size=0.2, random_state=7, stratify=df["churned"]
)
model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=7).fit(X_train, y_train)
auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1])
print(f"holdout AUC: {auc:.3f}")

KNOWN_AT_SCORING = {"tenure_months", "monthly_spend", "support_tickets"}
for col in features:
    assert col in KNOWN_AT_SCORING, f"availability audit: '{col}' unknown at scoring time"
print(f"availability audit passed: {len(features)} features known at scoring time")
traceback:
holdout AUC: 0.998
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 20
     18 KNOWN_AT_SCORING = {"tenure_months", "monthly_spend", "support_tickets"}

---> 20     assert col in KNOWN_AT_SCORING, f"availability audit: '{col}' unknown at scoring time"

AssertionError: availability audit: 'days_until_churn' unknown at scoring time
correctOutput:
holdout AUC: 0.741
availability audit passed: 3 features known at scoring time

options.label: Add days_until_churn to the KNOWN_AT_SCORING registry — the warehouse clearly has the column
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

df = pd.read_csv("churn_snapshot.csv", parse_dates=["snapshot_date", "churn_date"])
horizon = df["churn_date"].fillna(df["snapshot_date"]) - df["snapshot_date"]
df["days_until_churn"] = horizon.dt.days
features = ["tenure_months", "monthly_spend", "support_tickets", "days_until_churn"]

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    df[features], df["churned"], test_size=0.2, random_state=7, stratify=df["churned"]
)
model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=7).fit(X_train, y_train)
auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1])
print(f"holdout AUC: {auc:.3f}")

KNOWN_AT_SCORING = {"tenure_months", "monthly_spend", "support_tickets", "days_until_churn"}
for col in features:
    assert col in KNOWN_AT_SCORING, f"availability audit: '{col}' unknown at scoring time"
print(f"availability audit passed: {len(features)} features known at scoring time")
options.resultLog:
holdout AUC: 0.998
availability audit passed: 4 features known at scoring time
options.rationale: Everything is green, which is exactly the problem: you edited the guardrail instead of the leak. The warehouse has `churn_date` only because it records outcomes *after they happen* — for every live customer the scoring service will ever see, that field is NaT and `days_until_churn` computes to 0, the value the model learned means "stays". Deployed, this model scores near-zero churn risk for the entire customer base, the win-back campaign targets nobody, and the 0.998 stays framed on the wall while retention burns. An audit you whitelist around is a rubber stamp.

options.label: Drop the label-derived feature; train only on columns knowable before the prediction cutoff
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

df = pd.read_csv("churn_snapshot.csv", parse_dates=["snapshot_date", "churn_date"])
features = ["tenure_months", "monthly_spend", "support_tickets"]

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    df[features], df["churned"], test_size=0.2, random_state=7, stratify=df["churned"]
)
model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=7).fit(X_train, y_train)
auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1])
print(f"holdout AUC: {auc:.3f}")

KNOWN_AT_SCORING = {"tenure_months", "monthly_spend", "support_tickets"}
for col in features:
    assert col in KNOWN_AT_SCORING, f"availability audit: '{col}' unknown at scoring time"
print(f"availability audit passed: {len(features)} features known at scoring time")
options.resultLog:
holdout AUC: 0.741
availability audit passed: 3 features known at scoring time
options.rationale: `days_until_churn` was arithmetic on `churn_date`, an outcome timestamp the pipeline writes only after the event being predicted — the label re-encoded as a number. Removing it restores the invariant the audit enforces: every input is computable at the prediction cutoff, before the outcome exists. The drop from 0.998 to 0.741 is not a regression; it is the model's first honest measurement, and the right baseline to improve with genuinely pre-cutoff signals like usage recency or spend trajectory.

options.label: Regularize the booster (fewer, shallower trees) — 0.998 AUC means the model is overfitting
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

df = pd.read_csv("churn_snapshot.csv", parse_dates=["snapshot_date", "churn_date"])
horizon = df["churn_date"].fillna(df["snapshot_date"]) - df["snapshot_date"]
df["days_until_churn"] = horizon.dt.days
features = ["tenure_months", "monthly_spend", "support_tickets", "days_until_churn"]

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    df[features], df["churned"], test_size=0.2, random_state=7, stratify=df["churned"]
)
model = GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=40, max_depth=2, random_state=7).fit(X_train, y_train)
auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1])
print(f"holdout AUC: {auc:.3f}")

KNOWN_AT_SCORING = {"tenure_months", "monthly_spend", "support_tickets"}
for col in features:
    assert col in KNOWN_AT_SCORING, f"availability audit: '{col}' unknown at scoring time"
print(f"availability audit passed: {len(features)} features known at scoring time")
options.resultLog:
holdout AUC: 0.997
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 20
     18 KNOWN_AT_SCORING = {"tenure_months", "monthly_spend", "support_tickets"}
     19 for col in features:
---> 20     assert col in KNOWN_AT_SCORING, f"availability audit: '{col}' unknown at scoring time"

AssertionError: availability audit: 'days_until_churn' unknown at scoring time
options.rationale: Overfitting is the model memorizing noise; this is the model reading signal — the truest signal in the table, because the feature *is* the label. A depth-2 tree needs one split on `days_until_churn` to separate the classes, so forty shallow trees score 0.997 just like five hundred deep ones would. Regularization shrinks variance; it cannot un-know information that was baked into the features. And the audit, which checks the feature list rather than the model, fails on exactly the same line.

hints: An AUC of 0.998 on customer churn is not a breakthrough — it is the metric telling you the test rows carry their own answers. Apply the deployment question to each feature: at the instant the scoring service must produce a probability for a live customer, could this value already exist anywhere?

Lines 7–9: `days_until_churn` is arithmetic on `churn_date`, a column the pipeline writes only after the outcome you are predicting has happened (and leaves NaT while a customer stays). Compare the feature list on line 9 against the scoring registry on line 18 — one entry can never exist in both worlds.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
`churn_date` is an outcome column: the pipeline writes it when a customer churns and leaves it NaT while they stay. Line 7's `fillna(df["snapshot_date"])` therefore maps every retained customer to a horizon of exactly 0 days and every churned customer to a positive one — `days_until_churn` is the label, re-encoded as arithmetic. The gradient booster needs a single split to read the answer back out, which is why the holdout AUC lands at 0.998: every test row carries its own ground truth in a feature column. Nothing about the split, the model, or the metric is wrong; the *table* is wrong, and the availability audit on line 20 is the only cell positioned to notice.

### Why the fix is correct
Dropping the derived column restores the invariant the audit encodes: every model input must be knowable at the moment of prediction, before the outcome exists. A live customer being scored today has no `churn_date` yet — the warehouse can only produce that value by looking into the future, which historical training data can do and production never can. The honest AUC of 0.741 is the model's true skill, and the correct baseline to improve with features computed strictly from pre-cutoff history.
recruiterReview: Correct call — and the discipline matters more than the diff. You treated 0.998 as an indictment rather than an achievement, traced `days_until_churn` back to an outcome timestamp, and accepted the honest 0.741 instead of negotiating with the audit. That last part is the real test: the tempting move was whitelisting the feature, which turns a guardrail into a rubber stamp. The principle is prediction-time availability — every feature must be computable strictly before the outcome window opens. Next step: move this audit out of the notebook and into CI backed by point-in-time joins, so the cutoff is enforced by machinery instead of memory. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Target Leakage: When a Feature Is the Answer Wearing a Costume

Target leakage is the failure mode where information about the outcome you are predicting flows into the features you predict it from. The model does not learn to forecast; it learns to *transcribe*. Offline metrics look spectacular, stakeholders celebrate, and the deployed model collapses — because the one place the leaked information never exists is production, at prediction time, before the outcome has happened.

### The prediction-time test
Every feature must pass one question: **at the instant the model is asked for a prediction, could this value already be known?** Not "is it in the warehouse" — warehouses are retrospective and full of the future. Known *then*, for *that* row. A churn model scores customers who have not churned; a default model scores loans still being repaid; a readmission model scores patients walking out the door. Any column whose value depends on how the story ends — an outcome timestamp, a closure code, a refund flag — fails the test, no matter how innocently arithmetic dresses it up.

### How the label sneaks in
Leakage rarely arrives as a column named `label_copy`. It arrives as a *derivation*: a duration computed against an outcome date, a count aggregated over a window that extends past the outcome, an account-status field that operations only fills in after the event. Join logic is a major vector — pulling "latest" values from a live table instead of values *as of* the training cutoff quietly imports months of future into every row. The defining trait of a leaked feature: it correlates with the label because it is *caused by* the label, not because it predicts it.

### Metrics as smoke detectors
A suspiciously good score is the cheapest leak detector you own. Tabular churn problems live around 0.70–0.85 AUC; 0.99+ means investigate, not deploy. Two follow-ups make the detector actionable. First, rank feature importances and interrogate the top one — leaks dominate importance charts, because they are the strongest signal in the table. Second, ablate: retrain without the suspect and watch the metric. A feature whose removal costs 25 points of AUC was not a feature; it was the answer key.

### Defenses that scale
Notebook vigilance does not survive team growth, so durable defenses are structural. Maintain an explicit registry of features the scoring service can actually serve, and gate training on it — the audit pattern in this challenge. Build training sets with point-in-time joins, so each row sees only data stamped before its own cutoff. Backtest the way you will serve: train on months 1–6, score month 7, and any feature that requires the future simply fails to materialize. The shared principle: make time travel a build error, not a code-review hope.

### Common variations of this bug
- Durations or flags derived from outcome timestamps: `days_until_churn`, `time_to_default`, `resolved_within_sla`.
- Post-outcome operational columns: `refund_issued`, `cancellation_reason`, `collections_stage`, discharge notes.
- Aggregation windows that overlap the outcome period, e.g. "30-day activity" computed over days after the churn event.
- Target encoding or group statistics computed with the row's own label included.
- Joins without point-in-time semantics — taking the latest warehouse value instead of the value as of the prediction cutoff.
- Train/serve skew twins: a feature richly populated in historical tables but NULL or zero for every live row the model actually scores.
